coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-Ag nanoparticle complex as a highly sensitive “turn-on” fluorescent probe for hydrogen sulfide: A DFT/TD-DFT study of electronic transitions and mechanism of sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chao
Ding, Yongqi
Bi, Xinyu
Luo, Jingxuan
Wang, Guo
Lin, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description: We present an efficient carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-modified silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) (CQDs-AgNPs) as turn-on fluorescence nanoprobe for monitoring hydrogen sulfide (H2S) in brain microdialysate. The fluorescence of the CQDs-AgNPs probe can be selectively and sensitively turned on by H2S due to the formation of AgS bonds between AgNPs and H2S and breaking of the AgN bond between AgNPs and CQDs. Density functional theory (DFT)/time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) investigation were thoroughly conducted on the electronic structures, absorption and emission spectra of CQDs-AgNPs complex as well as the sensing mechanism for hydrogen sulfide. The CQDs-AgNPs fluorescent probe illustrates a wide linear detection range towards H2S from 1 to 1900 nM with the detection limit of ∼0.4 nM. This improved sensitivity, lower limit, along with the high selectivity and fast response toward H2S, makes this CQDs-AgNPs complex successfully monitor H2S basal level in rats without exogenous stimulation, which was calculated to be 3.08 ± 0.10 μM (n = 3). This novel fluorescence probe provides an assay for direct detection of H2S in the cerebral systems and contributes to understand the physiological function and molecular mechanisms of endogenous H2S.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrogen sulfide
DFT/TD-DFT
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detections of H2S using the CQDs-AgNPs probe
In vivo microdialysis and H2S sensing
The fluorescence responses of H2S sensing with CQDs-AgNPs probe
H2S sensing in rat brain microdialysates during the cerebral calm/ischemia

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed mechanism of the CQDs-AgNPs probe for H2S sensing.

TEM image of CQDs-AgNPs probe in the absence (A) and presence (B) of H2S. UV–vis absorption spectra (C) of Tris (a), CQDs (b), AgNPs (c), and CQDs-AgNPs probe (d). Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (D): the excitation spectra of CQDs (a); the emission spectrum of CQDs excited at 350 nm (b); the excitation spectra of CQDs-AgNPs (c) and the emission spectrum of CQDs-AgNPs excited at 350 nm (d).

Molecular orbitals involved in (a) the absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra for the CQDs, molecular orbitals involved in (c) the absorption and (d) fluorescence spectra for the CQDs connected to the silver atom.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs-AgNPs probe with difference concentration of Na2S solutions (from bottom to up: 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 800, 1900 nM, respectively). The inset in (a) shows the fluorescence spectra of Na2S concentration range from bottom to up: 1–800 nM. (B) The curve of fluorescence restoration evaluated with (F1-F0)/F0 vs H2S concentration range from 1 to 1900 nM. The inset in (B) is the linear calibration plot for H2S detection. (C) The fluorescence intensity ratios of CQDs-AgNPs probe with different metal ions, anions and biological molecules (from 1 to 23 were L-Cys, GSH, AA, DA, DOPAC, UA, glucose, H2O2, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 −, Na2S, respectively). F0 and F1 refer to the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs-AgNPs at 440 nm in the absence and presence of series of Na2S solutions or interferences, respectively. Each point represents mean ± SD (n = 3).

Detection of H2S by CQDs-AgNPs probe in the microdialysate (diluted 10 times) following rat brain cerebral clam/ischemia. The brain microdialysates were collected every 30 μL of the cerebral calm/ischemia process. Error bars represent the SD of replicates measurements with n = 3.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-Ag nanoparticle complex as a highly sensitive “turn-on” fluorescent probe for hydrogen sulfide: A DFT/TD-DFT study of electronic transitions and mechanism of sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present an efficient carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-modified silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) (CQDs-AgNPs) as turn-on fluorescence nanoprobe for monitoring hydrogen sulfide (H2S) in brain microdialysate. The fluorescence of the CQDs-AgNPs probe can be selectively and sensitively turned on by H2S due to the formation of AgS bonds between AgNPs and H2S and breaking of the AgN bond between AgNPs and CQDs. Density functional theory (DFT)/time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) investigation were thoroughly conducted on the electronic structures, absorption and emission spectra of CQDs-AgNPs complex as well as the sensing mechanism for hydrogen sulfide. The CQDs-AgNPs fluorescent probe illustrates a wide linear detection range towards H2S from 1 to 1900 nM with the detection limit of ∼0.4 nM. This improved sensitivity, lower limit, along with the high selectivity and fast response toward H2S, makes this CQDs-AgNPs complex successfully monitor H2S basal level in rats without exogenous stimulation, which was calculated to be 3.08 ± 0.10 μM (n = 3). This novel fluorescence probe provides an assay for direct detection of H2S in the cerebral systems and contributes to understand the physiological function and molecular mechanisms of endogenous H2S.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is an endogenous gaseous signaling molecule in addition to nitric oxide and carbon monoxide. It has important physiological and pathological roles in living systems [1–6]. Low concentrations of H2S induce distress while high levels can cause unconsciousness, permanent brain damage, or even cell death through asphyxiation [7]. Thus, rapid, simple, and highly sensitive detection methods are needed including chemosensor in aqueous media for biological systems. This can explain the molecular mechanisms underlying H2S. Various assay strategies have been designed to monitor H2S in vitro including electroanalytical assays, as well as electroluminescent, high performance liquid chromatography, and colorimetric detection schemes [8–11].

Fluorescence methods are particularly powerful for the sensitive detection of targets because of their high sensitivity and specificity and easily accessible instrumentation [12–18]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been described as a new quasi-zero-dimensional material [19,20]. Versus metallic quantum dots, CQDs have low synthetic cost, low toxicity, chemical inertness, and good water solubility. They are easily functionalized and are good electron donors and acceptors. They are a novel platform for fluorescence biosensors, bio-imaging, drug carriers, and reaction catalysts [21]. CQDs have optical and electronic properties [22–24], and they are excellent fluorophores. They can be quenched by ions, hydroxyls, or other nanoparticles etc. This facilitates fluorescent sensing [25–27]. Our group has used CQDs to fabricate sensors for Fe3+ [28], Cu2+ [29] and ascorbic acid (AA) [26,30]. Zhang’s group used nitrogen-doped CQDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) to detect cysteine [27]. Other “turn-off” CQD-based sensing systems for sulfide detection have also been designed. For instance, Yu’s group described a CQDs-based FRET ratiometric hydrogen sulfide sensor by conjugating a naphthalimideazide derivative as the sulfide sensitive and selective probe [31]. Abdollahi et al. developed a two-channel fluorescence probe for the indirect determination of sulfide ions based on highly fluorescent CQDs [32]. Metal ions or other substances can also quench CQD fluorescence, and thus fluorescent probes are susceptible to other interfering substances. This affects their selectivity and sensitivity. Therefore, sensitive and selective CQD-based probes for the detection of H2S in brain systems are still urgently needed.

In this communication, as illustrated in Scheme 1 , we first synthesized 3.15 nm N-doped CQDs by pyrolysis of Tris (i.e., tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane). The CQDs and AgNPs are connected to each other through AgN bond accompanied by the fluorescence quenching via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), forming the CQDs-AgNPs fluorescent nanoprobe. The fluorescence of the CQDs-AgNPs probe can be sensitively recovered by H2S to give an “turn-on” fluorescence response. Furthermore, the CQDs-AgNPs fluorescent probe illustrates a wide linear range from 1 to 1900 nM with the detection limit of 0.4 nM. As far as we know, this is one of the most sensitive CQDs-based fluorescent methods for H2S detection. This CQDs-AgNPs probe with high sensitivity allows to successfully determining the cortical H2S level in rat brain during the calm/ischemia process, which reveals potential applications in clinical medicine examination and disease diagnosis.

Tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris), ascorbic acid (AA), l-cysteine (L-Cys), l-glutathione oxidized (GSH), dopamine (DA), 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC) and uric acid (UA) were all purchased from Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Other chemicals were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Unless otherwise stated, all reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received, and all aqueous solutions were prepared with double-distilled water. Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100F Transmission Electron Microscope (Japan) operated at 200 kV. Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDX) was characterized by FE-SEM (SU8010, Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were accomplished by Equinox55 spectrophotometer (BRUKER, Germany). Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were gained by using a 1 cm path length quartz cell at room temperature based on using a Fluorescence F-4500 (Hitachi, Japan) and UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively.

The nitrogen-doped CQDs were synthesized through pyrolysis carbonization method [32]. In short, 1 g of Tris powder were heated at 230 °C in a beaker for 20 min, then dissolved and diluted with double-distilled water immediately to about 120 mL (approximately 8.33 mg/mL) to obtain the strongest fluorescence nitrogen-doped CQDs. The quantum yield (QY) measurements were summarized in Table S1 (provided in the supplemental material. On the other hand, NaBH4 solution (10 mL and 2 mM) was slowly added to AgNO3 solution (40 mL and 1 mM), magnetic stirred for 15 min with 0 °C ice water bath. For the hybridizing process, the CQDs solutions (pH to be 7.0) were mixed with AgNPs solution with the volume ratio of 1:1 at room temperature and sonicated for 5 min. Finally, the color of the solution turn to dark orange and the CQDs-AgNPs probe was synthesized successfully. The CQDs-AgNPs probe solutions were stored under 4 °C before further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of H2S using the CQDs-AgNPs probe
ce:section.ce:para: After chosen the optimal experimental conditions (the ratio of probe, pH, and temperature), different concentrations (1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 800, and 1900 nM) of Na2S solution were added in CQDs-AgNPs probe at room temperature. After incubated for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were measured at 350 nm excitation to obtain the linear relationship between FL intensity value and the concentrations of H2S. Furthermore, some metal ions, anions and biological molecules were investigated to explore the selectivity of the probe (metal ions, anions and biomolecules: L-Cys (100 nM); GSH (100 nM); AA (10 μM); DA (200 nM); DOPAC (1 μM); UA (10 μM); glucose (10 μM); H2O2 (1 μM); K+ (2.4 mM); Na+ (128 mM); Ca2+ (100 μM); Mg2+ (1.5 μM); Fe3+ (10 μM); Co2+ (2 μM); Ni2+ (2 μM); Cu2+ (5 μM); Zn2+ (5 μM); HPO4 2− (10 μM); H2PO4 − (10 μM); SO4 2− (10 μM); HCO3 − (10 μM); NO3 − (10 μM) and standard Na2S solution (100 nM) as control group).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo microdialysis and H2S sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Microdialysis experiments in vivo were conducted as previous reports [33]. Briefly, the rats inserted with guide tube were anesthetized by chloral hydrate (350 mg/kg i.p.). The microdialysis probe (2 mm in length, Bioanalytical Systems Inc. (BAS), BAS Carnegie Medicine) was implanted into the brain cortex region and was perfused with artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF) (1.1 mM CaCl2, 0.5 mM Na2SO4, 126 mM NaCl, 0.85 mM MgCl2, 0.5 mM KH2PO4, 2.4 mM KCl, 27.5 mM NaHCO3, pH 7.0) at a flow rate of 3 μL/min. After equilibrated for 90 min by continuously perfused aCSF through the microdialysis probe, every 30 μL of brain microdialysate was collected for analysis. According to the previous reports, the H2S concentrations in brain microdialysates vary in several micromole levels [9]. As a result, in this study, the brain microdialysates sampled from rat cortex were first diluted ten times and then further detected by the CQDs-AgNPs probe. Detection of H2S in the microdialysates were conducted using the same experimental conditions as the linear investigation.

We first synthesized 3.15 nm N-doped CQDs (Fig. S1A) by pyrolysis of Tris. From FT-IR spectra of CQDs (Fig. S1D), a new peak at 1640 cm−1 corresponded to the CC extending of polycyclic aromatichydrocarbons [35], supporting the CQDs were synthesized successfully. As shown in TEM images, when CQDs connected with AgNPs (average diameter to be 15 nm) to form CQDs-AgNPs probe (Fig. 1 A), the aggregation phenomenon of AgNPs occurred with fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the fluorescence excitation peaks of both the CQDs and the CQDs-AgNPs occur at approximately 350 nm (Fig. 1D), which nicely fits in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1C). The emission peaks of both at 440 nm were used for detection. The CC bond peak for the CQDs-AgNPs probe was shifted by 23 cm−1 to 1663 cm−1 due to mesomeric effects relative to the CQD peak. The peak at ∼3338 cm−1 is attributed to AgN stretching vibrations. The results indicate that the CQDs-AgNPs probe was prepared successfully.

To further explore the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of the formed CQDs-AgNPs probe, theoretical calculations were performed based on time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) in order to explain the reduced fluorescence signal of the CQDs when connected to AgNPs. Since the CQDs are an aromatic system that contains COH, CH2 OH groups and −NH2 groups [26], a simple model as shown in Fig. 2 a was built to give qualitative description of the CQDs. When CQDs are connected to AgNPs, AgN bonds are formed. A model as shown in Fig. 2c with an Ag atom bonded to the N atom was built. In the calculation, the sophisticated hybrid density functional B3LYP was adopted. The Lanl2DZ basis set for Ag and 6–31G (d) basis set for all the other elements were used. The absorption spectra were calculated under the geometries of the ground states, while the fluorescence spectra were obtained under those of the corresponding excited states.

For the CQDs, the highest absorption spectrum peak is at 325.37 nm with oscillator strength (f) of 0.6616, while the highest fluorescence spectrum peak is 440.87 nm (f = 0.1830). When the CQDs are connected to the silver atom, the above two peaks become 300.34 nm (f = 0.3518) and 447.16 nm (f = 0.1256), respectively, those are consistent with Fig. 1C and D. The CQDs-AgNPs probe was obtained by directly mixing AgNPs and CQDs, and the system color changes from yellow to pink because the AgNPs and CQDs were connected through Ag-N bond combination due to and the distance between CQDs and AgNPs become smaller. In addition, for the absorption spectra of CQDs and AgNPs (Fig. 1C), some of the absorption peaks overlap which pave the way for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The wavelengths for these simple models agree well with the experimental values, especially for the absorption spectra. Furthermore, the shift of the peaks after connected to the silver is quite small (less than 7 nm), which is consistent with the experiment. After connected to the silver atom, the oscillator strength for the fluorescence spectrum of the carbon dot reduces from 0.1830 to 0.1256, which explains the reduced fluorescence signal in the experiment.

In order to further elucidate the reason why the oscillator strength reduces, molecular orbitals involved in the electronic transitions are analysed. Attribution of the spectrum peaks is described in supporting information and the absorption spectrum peak at 325.37 nm for the CQDs is attributed to nine transitions. The transition with the largest coefficient is from the HOMO to LUMO + 5. For the other three spectrum peaks, the transitions are different. It is difficult to give a comparison between different molecular orbitals.

Hence, the transitions involving the HOMOs are discussed in detail. The corresponding molecular orbitals are shown in Fig. 2, which should give intuitive picture of the transitions. In Fig. 2, all the orbitals have π characteristics, indicating that the transitions are between π and π*. It is noted that the HOMOs in Fig. 2a and b are similar. The reason is that the geometries of the ground and excited states have little difference, due to the confinement of the aromatic hydrocarbon skeleton. It is also similar for the HOMOs in Fig. 2c and d. The molecular orbitals are almost uniformly distributed on the carbon atoms, except for the HOMOs in Fig. 2c and d. These two HOMOs are not uniform. There is big contribution from the silver atom, but little from some carbon atoms on one side. In contrast, there is little contribution from the silver atom for the LUMO + 8 and LUMO + 4. For the CQDs connected to the silver atom, the transition from the HOMO to LUMO + 8 as shown in Fig. 2c and that from the LUMO + 4 to HOMO as shown in Fig. 2d require charge redistribution between the silver atom and some carbon atoms. Therefore, the oscillator strength of the CQDs reduces after connected to the silver atom. These explain the reduced fluorescence signal in the experiment.

It is well known that sulfur-containing compounds can strongly bind silver nanoparticles. Based on the above simple models, we assumed the reaction to be AgCQD + H2SHCQD + AgHS. The energy change is −39.8 kcal/mol indicating that the reaction is energetically favorable. The CQDs are released after the reaction to recover fluorescence. Only CQDs-AgNPs probe can lead to obvious fluorescence recovery (Fig. S3A). The underlying mechanism was proved to be between the H2S and CQDs-AgNPs. When Na2S was added to the CQDs-AgNPs probe solution, the Na2S was converted to H2S at pH 7.0 [9]. This is suitable with the pH needed for H2S detection (Fig. S3B). TEM images (Fig. 1A–B) show that the AgNPs and H2S bound via AgS bonds. Some of the Ag-N bonds broke between the CQDs and the AgNPs. This is because the binding capacity of the Ag-S bonds is stronger than the AgN bonds. Consequently, some of the CQDs were newly released, and the AgNPs were newly dispersed from the agglomeration. This restored the CQDs-AgNPs fluorescence. The EDX spectra further indicated the presence of carbon and silver in the CQDs-AgNPs probe (Fig. S2A). The EDX spectrum of the H2S with CQDs-AgNPs (Fig. S2B) showed the effects of nitrogen and sulfur on the luminescence mechanism of the CQDs-AgNPs probe. Therefore, a rapid and highly sensitive analysis tool was established for the detection of H2S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence responses of H2S sensing with CQDs-AgNPs probe
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs-AgNPs probe was optimized at pH 7.0 (Fig. S3B), a volume ratio of 1:1 (Fig. S4A), and room temperature (Fig. S4B). At these conditions, 1–1900 nM of Na2S in 100 μL was added to 1 mL of CQDs-AgNPs probe. The fluorescence values of the CQDs-AgNPs at 440 nm gradually increased (Fig. 3 A). Fig. 3B illustrates the relationship of the fluorescence restoration ratio (F1-F0)/F0 with different H2S concentrations. The inset of Fig. 3B shows a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.997) from 1 to 1900 nM ((F1-F0)/F0 = 0.0424 + 0.0037 [H2S]/nM) with a detection limit of ∼0.4 nM (S/N = 3). Versus the previously reported H2S fluorescent probes (Table S2), the current CQDs-AgNPs probe was very sensitive, selective, and had a good lower detection limit. Moreover, these highly sensitive sensors allow sufficient sample dilution during the assay to effectively reduce interference from the complicated matrix (physiological species) [34]. This is critical for sensing cerebral H2S.

To explore probe selectivity, the CQDs-AgNPs probe was studied in the presence of interferences. The highly selective nature of the probe is due to the combination of the AgNPs with H2S to generate Ag-S and then release CQDs for signal generation. Fig. 3C demonstrates that only H2S elicits an obvious response from the CQDs-AgNPs probe due to the high selectivity of the CQDs-AgNPs probe for H2S in aqueous solution. This also indicates that the method can detect H2S in real biological environments or samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2S sensing in rat brain microdialysates during the cerebral calm/ischemia
ce:section.ce:para: We next evaluated this tool in a rat cerebral calm/ischemia experimental model. The cerebral microdialysates containing H2S were obtained by microdialysis in pre- and post-ischemia. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs-AgNPs probe with brain microdialysates (diluted 10 times) was measured. Fig. 4 shows that before ischemia (60 min), the basal level of H2S in rat brain cortical was 3.08 ± 0.10 μM; this increased to 4.67 ± 0.07 μM (n = 3) with ischemia. These values are similar to values previously reported [35] indicating that the level of H2S in the ischemic state increases to provide further data on neurotoxicity. These results suggest that the fluorescent probe has high selectivity and sensitivity for H2S. It can be used in animal models for practical applications.

In summary, we developed a novel and efficient method to combine CQDs and AgNPs complexes as a fluorescence probe for H2S detection. The sensing mechanism was validated in a DFT/TD-DFT study. The fluorescence assay was used to measure cortical H2S in rat brain during ischemia. This validated its potential applications in clinical medicine and disease diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation (21575090), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (2162009), Scientific Research Project of Beijing Educational Committee (KM201810028008), and Youth Innovative Research Team of Capital Normal University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.186.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen sulfide: a neuromodulator and neuroprotectant in the central nervous system

Small-molecule two-photon probes for bioimaging applications
A new gaseous signaling molecule emerges: cardioprotective role of hydrogen sulfide
Hydrogen sulfide attenuates myocardial ischemia-reperfusion injury by preservation of mitochondrial function
Reduction of ammine ruthenium (III) by sulfide enables in vivo electrochemical monitoring of free endogenous hydrogen sulfide
Reaction-based turn-on electrochemiluminescent sensor with a ruthenium [II] complex for selective detection of extracellular hydrogen sulfide in rat brain
Capture and visualization of hydrogen sulfide by a fluorescent probe
A ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid detection of hydrogen sulfide in mitochondria
A bright fluorescent probe for H2S enables analytic responsive: 3D imaging in live zebrafish using light sheet fluorescence microscopy
Electrophilic cyanate as a recognition motif for reactive sulfur species: selective fluorescence detection of H2S
Fluorescent probes for sensing and imaging biological hydrogen sulfide
Mitochondria-targeted reaction-based fluorescent probe for hydrogen sulfide
Visualization of in vivo hydrogen sulfide production by a bioluminescence probe in cancer cells and nude mice
Ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive biosensing and bioimaging
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the sensitive detection of Bacillus thuringiensis transgenic sequence based on silica coated supermagnetic nanoparticles and quantum dots
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
In vivo fluorescence imaging and the diagnosis of stem cells using quantum dots for regenerative medicine
Carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster nanosatellite for ratiometric fluorescence probe and imaging for hydrogen peroxide in living cells
Simultaneous stripping determination of cadmium and lead ions based on the N-doped carbon quantum dots-graphene oxide hybrid
Development of a carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent Cu2+ probe suitable for living cell imaging
Hexagonal cobalt oxyhydroxide-carbon dots hybridized surface: high sensitive fluorescence turn-on probe for monitoring of ascorbic acid in rat brain following brain ischemia
Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped and amino acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots from glycine, and their application to the fluorometric determination of ferric ion
Tunable fluorescent silica-coated carbon dots: a synergistic effect for enhancing the fluorescence sensing of extracellular Cu2+ in rat brain
Fe3+-functionalized carbon quantum dots: a facile preparation strategy and detection for ascorbic acid in rat brain microdialysates
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting hydrogen sulfide in aqueous media and inside live cells
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Dynamic regional changes of extracellular ascorbic acid during global cerebral ischemia: studied with in vivo microdialysis coupled with on-line electrochemical detection
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
A fluorescent probe for rapid detection of hydrogen sulfide in blood plasma and brain tissues in mice

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chao Wang received his B.S. in department of chemistry from Capital Normal University, China in 2017. He is now a graduate student in department of chemistry at Capital Normal University. His research focuses on fluorescence nanoparticles and biomolecules detection.

Yongqi Ding received his B.S. in college of chemistry and chemical engineering from Shangqiu Normal University, China in 2016. He is now a graduate student in department of chemistry at Capital Normal University. His research focuses on colorimetric and fluorescence detection methods of biomolecules.

Xinyu Bi is now an undergraduate student in department of chemistry at Capital Normal University, China. His research focuses on fluorescence detection methods and fluorescence materials design toward biomolecules.
Jingxuan Luo received his B.S. in department of chemistry from Capital Normal University, China in 2017. His research focuses on fluorescence detection methods of biomolecules.

Guo Wang received his PhD degree in chemistry from Beijing Normal University, China in 2009. He joined as a lecturer in department of chemistry at Capital Normal University in 2009, and currently is working as an associate professor. His research focuses on the relationship between structure and properties of materials from a quantum chemistry perspective.

Yuqing Lin is currently an professor at Capital Normal University. She received her B.S. in 2003 from Zhengzhou University (China) and her Ph.D.in Analytical Chemistry from Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science (ICCAS) in 2008. She worked as an Assistant Professor in the field of in vivo and online electroanalytical chemistry in ICCAS from 2008 to 2010 and finished her post-doctoral research in the department of chemistry at the University of Gothenburg (Sweden) from 2010 to 2012. Her research interests include micro/nanostructured electroanalytical and fluorescent methods for probing neurochemistry both in vivo and in single cells.
